nodes.scenario: Which HTTP client should we use to call the Anthropic API from Elixir?

variants.decision: Req: modern Elixir HTTP client, declarative API, built-in JSON handling

nodes.scenario: When multiple participants submit scenario reframings in rapid succession, should each submission trigger a separate LLM synthesis call, or should we debounce?

variants.decision: 800ms debounce: each new submission resets the timer; LLM call fires only after 800ms of quiet; managed via `{:debounce, key, delay_ms, call_spec}` effect

nodes.scenario: How do we test decision flows that trigger LLM calls without making real API requests, while keeping the LLM integration swappable?

variants.decision: Elixir `@behaviour` on the LLM module; `MaudeLibs.LLM.Mock` implements canned responses; Server reads the module from application config at startup

nodes.scenario: Should the scaffolding LLM call (generating for/against points per option) be one call for all options or one call per option?

variants.decision: Single call: all options + priorities in one request; output array of {name, for, against} per option

nodes.scenario: How do we ensure LLM responses are machine-parseable JSON that maps predictably to our Elixir structs?

variants.decision: Prompt coercion: system prompt instructs model to return only raw JSON matching the specified schema; no tool use

nodes.scenario: How do invited participants discover and navigate to a decision they've been invited to join?

variants.decision: Per-user PubSub topic (`"user:#{username}"`) delivers invite notifications; canvas shows a modal; creator uses add/remove list UI with UserRegistry autocomplete

nodes.scenario: How does the invite UI know which usernames exist in the system so the creator can autocomplete when inviting participants?

variants.decision: ETS-backed GenServer that registers every username on /join; `list_usernames/0` feeds an HTML `<datalist>` for autocomplete

nodes.scenario: How does the Decision.Server broadcast state changes to all connected LiveViews for a given decision?

variants.decision: Broadcast full Decision struct on every state change; LiveViews assign and re-render

nodes.scenario: How do we identify participants across LiveView connections without building an authentication system?

variants.decision: Username stored in Plug session via POST /session; session cookie persists the identity; no database, no accounts, no passwords

nodes.scenario: How do non-participants (observers, dropped users) view a decision in progress without disrupting it?

variants.decision: Same decision_live.ex; check `username in decision.connected` to gate input controls; spectators see all but can't interact

nodes.scenario: How do we structure the decision state machine so it is testable, predictable, and easy to reason about under concurrent LiveView connections?

variants.decision: Pure Core + GenServer Shell: Core.handle/2 returns {:ok, decision, [effects]}, Server GenServer executes effects

nodes.scenario: When a participant's LiveView disconnects (page refresh, network hiccup, tab switch on mobile), should they be immediately removed from the connected set, or should there be a grace window for reconne

variants.decision: 5-second grace period: Server schedules a `{:disconnect_timeout, user}` message on disconnect; reconnect within 5s cancels the timer; configurable via `disconnect_grace_ms`

nodes.scenario: How do we handle a participant's LiveView disconnecting mid-decision without blocking the remaining participants?

variants.decision: connected + participants MapSets on Decision struct; terminate/2 triggers a 5-second grace period before removal; all ready-up checks use connected; participants persists across reconnects

nodes.scenario: How do we represent the current stage of a decision and its stage-specific data in Elixir?

variants.decision: Tagged struct per stage: %Stage.Lobby{}, %Stage.Scenario{}, etc., stored in Decision.stage

nodes.scenario: How many machines should we run on fly.io, and do we need BEAM clustering for LiveView state?

variants.decision: Single machine on fly.io (sjc region); auto-stop/auto-start; no clustering needed; all GenServer state on one node

nodes.scenario: Should decision state be persisted to a database, ETS, or live only in GenServer memory?

variants.decision: Ephemeral GenServers: all decision state in memory; accept state loss on restart for prototype scope

nodes.scenario: How do we position decision circles on the lobby canvas and participant cards on stage views so that layouts are non-overlapping, visually balanced, responsive to viewport size, and work within LiveVi

variants.decision: Three-layer architecture: (1) CSS fixed shell for persistent navigation, (2) D3 force simulation in JS hooks for position computation, (3) scale-to-fit transform on a virtual canvas for viewport adaptation. Two distinct D3 hooks serve the lobby canvas and stage views respectively.

Use d3-force in a LiveView JS hook for layout computation, with libgraph as the server-side graph data structure. Server sends node metadata (size, visibility, fixed position) via push_event; D3 handles physics and rendering.
Use d3-force via a Node.js port/NIF called from the server, broadcasting final positions via PubSub; client only animates
Do nothing - keep the current hand-rolled server-side force simulation
Rewrite the pure Elixir force layout as a tested, idempotent pure function using the spring-electrical model with adaptive step size and early termination

nodes.scenario: When multiple participants submit priorities or options, when should each participant's input become visible to the others?

variants.decision: All inputs visible live as you type; confirm button separates "I'm done" from "I'm ready to advance"

nodes.scenario: How should Claude's suggested priorities and options be presented and adopted by participants?

variants.decision: Toggle model: anyone can include/exclude a suggestion; last write wins; broadcast immediately

nodes.scenario: How do participants agree on the final scenario framing before advancing to priorities?

variants.decision: Unanimous vote: all connected participants must select the same candidate to advance

nodes.scenario: How should we structure UI components across the decision stages - reusable LiveComponents, inline function components, or duplication?

variants.decision: Inline function components (attr + slot, no stateful LiveComponent) for shared layout; per-stage inline heex for unique mechanics

nodes.scenario: Which CSS approach should we use for styling the Phoenix LiveView app?

variants.decision: Tailwind CSS + DaisyUI: Phoenix ships Tailwind by default; DaisyUI adds component classes (card, btn, badge, input) with custom dark/light themes in oklch

nodes.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?

variants.decision: Use both Dialyzer and the gradual type system during the transition period - maximum coverage now, converge to compiler-only when gradual types mature
Add `@spec`/`@type` annotations and run Dialyxir (`mix dialyzer`) for static type analysis
Lean into the compiler's gradual type system - let `mix compile` catch type errors, skip Dialyzer entirely
Do nothing - continue with `@callback` on the LLM behaviour and no other type annotations or checking
Add `@type` and `@spec` annotations to public functions for documentation, without running any enforcement tool

nodes.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?

variants.decision: Set threshold to 100% and add stage component modules to `ignore_modules`, unignoring them temporarily when modifying their code.
Set threshold to 99% and accept the `defmodule` gap without workarounds.
Do nothing - no coverage threshold enforcement.

nodes.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?

variants.decision: Use Wallaby (or Playwright via ports) to drive real browser sessions through the full multi-user UI workflow.
Dev seed routes for instant manual stage access + LiveView integration tests for automated multi-user regression coverage. Skip browser automation to avoid brittleness.
Dev-only seed routes that programmatically create decisions pre-advanced to any stage with fake users already joined.
Use Phoenix.LiveViewTest to mount LiveViews in-process, simulate multi-user flows, and assert stage transitions without a real browser.
Do nothing; continue with manual browser testing and existing Core unit tests.
Use Tidewave's MCP tools (project_eval, browser_eval, get_logs) as a live agentic verification layer during Claude Code sessions, complementing automated tests and seed routes.

nodes.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?

variants.decision: Migrate to Hammox - Mox with automatic typespec validation on mock return values via `@callback` specs
Hand-rolled mock modules - `LLM.Mock` and `LLM.ErrorMock` implement the LLM behaviour, swapped via `Application.get_env` config
Migrate to Mox - define a Mox mock from the LLM behaviour, use per-test expectations, remove hand-rolled mock modules
Do nothing - no mocking; tests call the real Anthropic API

api-wrapper.scenario: Which HTTP client should we use to call the Anthropic API from Elixir?

variants.scenario: Which HTTP client should we use to call the Anthropic API from Elixir?
variants.chosen_option: Req: modern Elixir HTTP client, declarative API, built-in JSON handling

reconsider.observe: Anthropic API changes (new models, new message format)
reconsider.respond: Thin wrapper means changes are localized to llm.ex; update in one place

debounced-calls.scenario: When multiple participants submit scenario reframings in rapid succession, should each submission trigger a separate LLM synthesis call, or should we debounce?

variants.scenario: When multiple participants submit scenario reframings in rapid succession, should each submission trigger a separate LLM synthesis call, or should we debounce?
variants.chosen_option: 800ms debounce: each new submission resets the timer; LLM call fires only after 800ms of quiet; managed via `{:debounce, key, delay_ms, call_spec}` effect

reconsider.observe: 800ms feels sluggish when only one participant is submitting
reconsider.respond: Reduce to 300-500ms; or fire immediately if only one connected user has submitted

reconsider.observe: Debounce timer accumulates across stages (key collision)
reconsider.respond: Namespace keys by stage or clear all debounce timers on stage transition

pluggable-behaviour.scenario: How do we test decision flows that trigger LLM calls without making real API requests, while keeping the LLM integration swappable?

variants.scenario: How do we test decision flows that trigger LLM calls without making real API requests, while keeping the LLM integration swappable?
variants.chosen_option: Elixir `@behaviour` on the LLM module; `MaudeLibs.LLM.Mock` implements canned responses; Server reads the module from application config at startup

reconsider.observe: Need to test error paths (LLM returns {:error, ...})
reconsider.respond: Add Mock variants or make Mock configurable per-test via process dictionary or Agent

reconsider.observe: Mock responses are too simplistic; don't exercise JSON parsing edge cases
reconsider.respond: Make Mock responses match exact API response shape, including nested structures

prompt-strategy.scenario: Should the scaffolding LLM call (generating for/against points per option) be one call for all options or one call per option?

variants.scenario: Should the scaffolding LLM call (generating for/against points per option) be one call for all options or one call per option?
variants.chosen_option: Single call: all options + priorities in one request; output array of {name, for, against} per option

reconsider.observe: One call times out due to large option count
reconsider.respond: Split into N parallel calls (one per option) and merge results

response-format.scenario: How do we ensure LLM responses are machine-parseable JSON that maps predictably to our Elixir structs?

variants.scenario: How do we ensure LLM responses are machine-parseable JSON that maps predictably to our Elixir structs?
variants.chosen_option: Prompt coercion: system prompt instructs model to return only raw JSON matching the specified schema; no tool use

reconsider.observe: Parse failures become frequent (>5% of calls)
reconsider.respond: Switch to Anthropic tool use / function calling for guaranteed JSON schema enforcement

invite-mechanism.scenario: How do invited participants discover and navigate to a decision they've been invited to join?

variants.scenario: How do invited participants discover and navigate to a decision they've been invited to join?
variants.chosen_option: Per-user PubSub topic (`"user:#{username}"`) delivers invite notifications; canvas shows a modal; creator uses add/remove list UI with UserRegistry autocomplete

reconsider.observe: Users miss invites because they're not on /canvas
reconsider.respond: Add a persistent "pending invites" badge visible from /d/:id

user-registry.scenario: How does the invite UI know which usernames exist in the system so the creator can autocomplete when inviting participants?

variants.scenario: How does the invite UI know which usernames exist in the system so the creator can autocomplete when inviting participants?
variants.chosen_option: ETS-backed GenServer that registers every username on /join; `list_usernames/0` feeds an HTML `<datalist>` for autocomplete

reconsider.observe: Stale usernames clutter autocomplete after many sessions
reconsider.respond: Add TTL-based cleanup (e.g., discard entries older than 24 hours)

reconsider.observe: Need to associate persistent data with users (history, preferences)
reconsider.respond: Graduate to a real user table with Ecto

pubsub-model.scenario: How does the Decision.Server broadcast state changes to all connected LiveViews for a given decision?

variants.scenario: How does the Decision.Server broadcast state changes to all connected LiveViews for a given decision?
variants.chosen_option: Broadcast full Decision struct on every state change; LiveViews assign and re-render

reconsider.observe: Broadcast payload becomes large (many options with long for/against text)
reconsider.respond: Switch to broadcasting only the changed stage field or a diff

session-identity.scenario: How do we identify participants across LiveView connections without building an authentication system?

variants.scenario: How do we identify participants across LiveView connections without building an authentication system?
variants.chosen_option: Username stored in Plug session via POST /session; session cookie persists the identity; no database, no accounts, no passwords

reconsider.observe: Username collisions cause confusion in decisions
reconsider.respond: Add uniqueness check against UserRegistry; show error on /join if taken

reconsider.observe: Need to persist decisions across sessions (e.g., "my decisions" page)
reconsider.respond: Add lightweight auth (magic link email, or OAuth) and associate decisions with accounts

spectator-view.scenario: How do non-participants (observers, dropped users) view a decision in progress without disrupting it?

variants.scenario: How do non-participants (observers, dropped users) view a decision in progress without disrupting it?
variants.chosen_option: Same decision_live.ex; check `username in decision.connected` to gate input controls; spectators see all but can't interact

reconsider.observe: Spectators want to interact (e.g., ask to join)
reconsider.respond: Add a "request to join" mechanic that creator can approve

core-architecture.scenario: How do we structure the decision state machine so it is testable, predictable, and easy to reason about under concurrent LiveView connections?

variants.scenario: How do we structure the decision state machine so it is testable, predictable, and easy to reason about under concurrent LiveView connections?
variants.chosen_option: Pure Core + GenServer Shell: Core.handle/2 returns {:ok, decision, [effects]}, Server GenServer executes effects

reconsider.observe: Effect list grows complex (debounce + async_llm + broadcast in one transition)
reconsider.respond: Add effect priority ordering or a dedicated effect pipeline module

disconnect-grace-period.scenario: When a participant's LiveView disconnects (page refresh, network hiccup, tab switch on mobile), should they be immediately removed from the connected set, or should there be a grace window for reconne

variants.scenario: When a participant's LiveView disconnects (page refresh, network hiccup, tab switch on mobile), should they be immediately removed from the connected set, or should there be a grace window for reconne
variants.chosen_option: 5-second grace period: Server schedules a `{:disconnect_timeout, user}` message on disconnect; reconnect within 5s cancels the timer; configurable via `disconnect_grace_ms`

reconsider.observe: 5 seconds feels too long; ghost presence confuses other participants
reconsider.respond: Reduce to 2-3 seconds; or add a visual "reconnecting..." indicator during grace period

reconsider.observe: 5 seconds feels too short; mobile users on slow networks time out
reconsider.respond: Increase to 10 seconds; consider exponential backoff for repeated disconnects

drop-handling.scenario: How do we handle a participant's LiveView disconnecting mid-decision without blocking the remaining participants?

variants.scenario: How do we handle a participant's LiveView disconnecting mid-decision without blocking the remaining participants?
variants.chosen_option: connected + participants MapSets on Decision struct; terminate/2 triggers a 5-second grace period before removal; all ready-up checks use connected; participants persists across reconnects

reconsider.observe: A decision gets stuck because all participants disconnected
reconsider.respond: Add a timeout or allow the last connected user to auto-advance after N minutes

stage-representation.scenario: How do we represent the current stage of a decision and its stage-specific data in Elixir?

variants.scenario: How do we represent the current stage of a decision and its stage-specific data in Elixir?
variants.chosen_option: Tagged struct per stage: %Stage.Lobby{}, %Stage.Scenario{}, etc., stored in Decision.stage

reconsider.observe: Stage structs need versioning for live deploys
reconsider.respond: Add a vsn field or use a registry-based approach

fly-config.scenario: How many machines should we run on fly.io, and do we need BEAM clustering for LiveView state?

variants.scenario: How many machines should we run on fly.io, and do we need BEAM clustering for LiveView state?
variants.chosen_option: Single machine on fly.io (sjc region); auto-stop/auto-start; no clustering needed; all GenServer state on one node

reconsider.observe: Demo requires high availability or multiple concurrent groups
reconsider.respond: Add libcluster + Horde for distributed GenServer registry; configure fly.io with 2+ machines and BEAM clustering

state-persistence.scenario: Should decision state be persisted to a database, ETS, or live only in GenServer memory?

variants.scenario: Should decision state be persisted to a database, ETS, or live only in GenServer memory?
variants.chosen_option: Ephemeral GenServers: all decision state in memory; accept state loss on restart for prototype scope

reconsider.observe: Demo requires state to survive deploys (multi-day event)
reconsider.respond: Add ETS persistence with :ets.tab2file on terminate and reload on start; or add Postgres/Ecto

force-layout-strategy.scenario: How do we position decision circles on the lobby canvas and participant cards on stage views so that layouts are non-overlapping, visually balanced, responsive to viewport size, and work within LiveVi

variants.scenario: How do we position decision circles on the lobby canvas and participant cards on stage views so that layouts are non-overlapping, visually balanced, responsive to viewport size, and work within LiveVi
variants.chosen_option: Three-layer architecture: (1) CSS fixed shell for persistent navigation, (2) D3 force simulation in JS hooks for position computation, (3) scale-to-fit transform on a virtual canvas for viewport adaptation. Two distinct D3 hooks serve the lobby canvas and stage views respectively.

reconsider.observe: LiveView morphdom patches cause visible position flicker despite StageForce.updated()
reconsider.respond: Add CSS transitions to card wrappers, or debounce re-apply

reconsider.observe: Stage participant count exceeds 6-8 and arc placement gets crowded
reconsider.respond: Switch stage simulation to use forceCollide in addition to forceX/forceY

reconsider.observe: Text becomes unreadable below 0.5x scale on small viewports
reconsider.respond: Add mobile-specific layout that stacks cards vertically instead of scaling

reconsider.observe: Want server-authoritative positions (e.g., screenshots)
reconsider.respond: Add periodic position sync from client back to server

variants.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
variants.chosen_option: Use d3-force in a LiveView JS hook for layout computation, with libgraph as the server-side graph data structure. Server sends node metadata (size, visibility, fixed position) via push_event; D3 handles physics and rendering.

reconsider.observe: LiveView hook lifecycle issues cause layout glitches on reconnect
reconsider.respond: Push full node list on mounted; hook re-runs simulation from scratch (fast - sub-second)

reconsider.observe: Need server-authoritative positions for a feature (e.g., server-side screenshot)
reconsider.respond: Add a periodic position sync from client back to server, or accept that screenshots show initial positions

reconsider.observe: Want to add edges between decisions later (e.g., dependencies)
reconsider.respond: libgraph on server models edges; pass links array to D3; add `forceLink()` to simulation

variants.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
variants.chosen_option: Use d3-force via a Node.js port/NIF called from the server, broadcasting final positions via PubSub; client only animates

reconsider.observe: Port communication latency exceeds the 1500ms tick budget
reconsider.respond: Move to client-side d3-force; server only tracks circle metadata

reconsider.observe: Node.js process crashes or hangs
reconsider.respond: Add supervision and restart logic; consider fallback to simple grid layout

variants.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
variants.chosen_option: Do nothing - keep the current hand-rolled server-side force simulation

reconsider.observe: A simple constant tweak finally produces stable behavior at 12+ nodes
reconsider.respond: Keep current approach; document the working constants

variants.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
variants.chosen_option: Rewrite the pure Elixir force layout as a tested, idempotent pure function using the spring-electrical model with adaptive step size and early termination

reconsider.observe: Tests pass but visual result looks wrong (clumped, uneven)
reconsider.respond: The force balance is wrong for this aesthetic goal; switch to d3-force client-side

reconsider.observe: O(n^2) computation exceeds tick budget at 20+ nodes
reconsider.respond: Reduce iterations or switch to client-side computation

reconsider.observe: Convergence tests are flaky due to floating point edge cases
reconsider.respond: Increase convergence threshold or use approximate equality

blind-mechanic.scenario: When multiple participants submit priorities or options, when should each participant's input become visible to the others?

variants.scenario: When multiple participants submit priorities or options, when should each participant's input become visible to the others?
variants.chosen_option: All inputs visible live as you type; confirm button separates "I'm done" from "I'm ready to advance"

reconsider.observe: Users complain of anchoring - their inputs are shaped by seeing others first
reconsider.respond: Switch to rolling reveal: hide inputs until user has confirmed their own entry

claude-suggestions.scenario: How should Claude's suggested priorities and options be presented and adopted by participants?

variants.scenario: How should Claude's suggested priorities and options be presented and adopted by participants?
variants.chosen_option: Toggle model: anyone can include/exclude a suggestion; last write wins; broadcast immediately

reconsider.observe: Last-write-wins causes confusion when two users rapidly toggle the same suggestion
reconsider.respond: Add optimistic locking or switch to majority vote (2/4 participants = include)

scenario-consensus.scenario: How do participants agree on the final scenario framing before advancing to priorities?

variants.scenario: How do participants agree on the final scenario framing before advancing to priorities?
variants.chosen_option: Unanimous vote: all connected participants must select the same candidate to advance

reconsider.observe: Participants are stuck and can't reach unanimity
reconsider.respond: Add a "creator override" escape hatch or a majority vote fallback after N minutes

component-strategy.scenario: How should we structure UI components across the decision stages - reusable LiveComponents, inline function components, or duplication?

variants.scenario: How should we structure UI components across the decision stages - reusable LiveComponents, inline function components, or duplication?
variants.chosen_option: Inline function components (attr + slot, no stateful LiveComponent) for shared layout; per-stage inline heex for unique mechanics

reconsider.observe: A component needs its own event handling (e.g., inline modal with close button)
reconsider.respond: Promote to stateful LiveComponent at that point only

css-framework.scenario: Which CSS approach should we use for styling the Phoenix LiveView app?

variants.scenario: Which CSS approach should we use for styling the Phoenix LiveView app?
variants.chosen_option: Tailwind CSS + DaisyUI: Phoenix ships Tailwind by default; DaisyUI adds component classes (card, btn, badge, input) with custom dark/light themes in oklch

reconsider.observe: Tailwind class names become unwieldy on complex components
reconsider.respond: Extract to Phoenix LiveComponents with @apply in component CSS files

type-checking.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?

variants.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
variants.chosen_option: Use both Dialyzer and the gradual type system during the transition period - maximum coverage now, converge to compiler-only when gradual types mature

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) covers cross-dependency inference and typed structs
reconsider.respond: Dialyzer is now redundant; drop Dialyxir, migrate `@spec` to new type signatures

reconsider.observe: The two tools produce conflicting or confusing feedback on the same code
reconsider.respond: Prioritize compiler warnings; Dialyzer becomes supplementary

reconsider.observe: Maintaining `@spec` annotations for Dialyzer while the gradual system infers types feels like double work
reconsider.respond: Stop adding new `@spec` annotations; let existing ones serve Dialyzer until it's dropped

variants.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
variants.chosen_option: Add `@spec`/`@type` annotations and run Dialyxir (`mix dialyzer`) for static type analysis

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) covers everything Dialyzer does at compile time
reconsider.respond: Drop Dialyxir dependency, migrate `@spec` to new type signature syntax, rely on compiler

reconsider.observe: Dialyzer misses a bug that the gradual type system would have caught
reconsider.respond: Accept the limitation or supplement with the gradual type system in parallel

reconsider.observe: PLT builds slow down the development workflow
reconsider.respond: Only run Dialyzer in precommit/CI, not on every save

variants.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
variants.chosen_option: Lean into the compiler's gradual type system - let `mix compile` catch type errors, skip Dialyzer entirely

reconsider.observe: v1.20 adds cross-dependency inference and guard types
reconsider.respond: Review warnings on upgrade; may surface new type issues in existing code

reconsider.observe: v1.21+ adds user-facing type signatures
reconsider.respond: Begin annotating public APIs with the new syntax for documentation and enforcement

reconsider.observe: The gradual type system misses bugs that Dialyzer would catch
reconsider.respond: Consider adding Dialyzer as a supplement until the gradual system reaches parity

variants.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
variants.chosen_option: Do nothing - continue with `@callback` on the LLM behaviour and no other type annotations or checking

reconsider.observe: A type-related bug reaches production that specs or the compiler would have caught
reconsider.respond: Adopt at least `@spec` annotations on public APIs

reconsider.observe: Elixir's gradual type system reaches maturity (v1.21+)
reconsider.respond: Adopt it with minimal migration cost since there's nothing to convert

variants.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
variants.chosen_option: Add `@type` and `@spec` annotations to public functions for documentation, without running any enforcement tool

reconsider.observe: Specs drift from actual code because nothing enforces them
reconsider.respond: Add Dialyzer to catch spec violations

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) introduces new type signature syntax
reconsider.respond: Migrate `@spec` annotations to the new syntax; specs-only approach makes this straightforward

coverage.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?

variants.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
variants.chosen_option: Set threshold to 100% and add stage component modules to `ignore_modules`, unignoring them temporarily when modifying their code.

reconsider.observe: Elixir's cover tool fixes the `defmodule` counting
reconsider.respond: Remove stage components from ignore_modules; 100% is achievable natively

reconsider.observe: Developers keep forgetting the unignore/re-ignore step and real gaps slip through
reconsider.respond: Drop to 99% threshold and remove stage components from ignore_modules

reconsider.observe: New stage components are added and the ignore_modules list keeps growing
reconsider.respond: Evaluate whether the bookkeeping cost outweighs the benefit of the 100% headline number

reconsider.observe: A CI script could automate the unignore check (run coverage once without ignore for each changed module)
reconsider.respond: Build the automation; this eliminates L2 workflow friction and L1 blind spot risk

variants.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
variants.chosen_option: Set threshold to 99% and accept the `defmodule` gap without workarounds.

reconsider.observe: Elixir's cover tool fixes the `defmodule` counting (e.g., stops marking it as a relevant line)
reconsider.respond: Bump threshold to 100%; the gap closes naturally

reconsider.observe: More modules with `defmodule`-only gaps accumulate, eroding the 99% buffer
reconsider.respond: Either add them to ignore_modules or lower threshold further - but this signals a code organization issue, not a coverage issue

reconsider.observe: A real bug hides in the 0.76% headroom
reconsider.respond: Tighten the threshold or switch to the 100% + ignore_modules approach

variants.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
variants.chosen_option: Do nothing - no coverage threshold enforcement.

reconsider.observe: A bug ships to production that would have been caught by even basic coverage enforcement
reconsider.respond: Revisit adding a threshold, even a low one like 80%

reconsider.observe: Test suite grows organically and maintains high coverage through culture alone
reconsider.respond: Status quo is working; formalize with a threshold only if drift appears

methodology.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Use Wallaby (or Playwright via ports) to drive real browser sessions through the full multi-user UI workflow.

reconsider.observe: Browser tests take >30s and block CI
reconsider.respond: Move to a separate "e2e" CI job that runs on merge only, not on every push

reconsider.observe: Tests break on every UI change despite behavior being correct
reconsider.respond: Switch to data-testid selectors and consider replacing with LiveViewTest for non-visual assertions

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Dev seed routes for instant manual stage access + LiveView integration tests for automated multi-user regression coverage. Skip browser automation to avoid brittleness.

reconsider.observe: JS hook or canvas bugs keep slipping through
reconsider.respond: Add targeted Wallaby tests for JS-heavy features only (not full flow coverage)

reconsider.observe: Seed route state shapes drift from reality after Core refactors
reconsider.respond: Add a test that validates each builder produces a state that Core.handle/2 accepts

reconsider.observe: Builder module grows complex with many stage permutations
reconsider.respond: Consider a pipeline API: `Builders.new(users) |> Builders.advance_to(:priorities)`

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Dev-only seed routes that programmatically create decisions pre-advanced to any stage with fake users already joined.

reconsider.observe: You find yourself manually testing the same flow repeatedly even with seeds
reconsider.respond: Add LiveView integration tests for that flow

reconsider.observe: Seed routes drift from real stage shapes after a Core refactor
reconsider.respond: Share builders between seeds and tests

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Use Phoenix.LiveViewTest to mount LiveViews in-process, simulate multi-user flows, and assert stage transitions without a real browser.

reconsider.observe: JS hooks or canvas interactions cause bugs that LiveViewTest can't catch
reconsider.respond: Add targeted Wallaby/Playwright tests for JS-heavy features only

reconsider.observe: Test setup for multi-user flows is too verbose
reconsider.respond: Extract shared builder/helper modules (similar to existing `decision()` helpers in core_test)

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Do nothing; continue with manual browser testing and existing Core unit tests.

reconsider.observe: Time spent manually testing exceeds time you'd spend writing test infrastructure
reconsider.respond: Adopt one of the other variants

reconsider.observe: A regression ships that automated tests would have caught
reconsider.respond: Prioritize automated coverage

variants.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
variants.chosen_option: Use Tidewave's MCP tools (project_eval, browser_eval, get_logs) as a live agentic verification layer during Claude Code sessions, complementing automated tests and seed routes.

reconsider.observe: You find yourself re-doing the same Tidewave verification steps across sessions
reconsider.respond: Encode those steps as LiveView integration tests so they persist

reconsider.observe: Tidewave snapshots become the only verification and tests are neglected
reconsider.respond: Tidewave is complementary, not a replacement. Run mix test before committing.

reconsider.observe: A new team member can't reproduce verification steps from a previous session
reconsider.respond: Document common verification patterns in a dev guide, or encode as tests

mock-strategy.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?

variants.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
variants.chosen_option: Migrate to Hammox - Mox with automatic typespec validation on mock return values via `@callback` specs

reconsider.observe: Server test suite grows large and `async: false` with `set_mox_global` becomes a bottleneck
reconsider.respond: Refactor test GenServer startup to use `Mox.allow/3` or start GenServer directly under test process, enabling per-process isolation and `async: true`

reconsider.observe: Elixir's gradual type system (v1.21+) covers `@callback` type signatures at compile time
reconsider.respond: Drop Hammox, swap `Hammox.expect` back to `Mox.expect`; the compiler now catches return type mismatches before tests even run. Hammox was a bridge for the gap year before compiler-native type checking.

reconsider.observe: Hammox maintenance lags behind Mox releases
reconsider.respond: Fall back to plain Mox; lose typespec validation but keep all other benefits

reconsider.observe: `@callback` specs are being phased out in favor of new type signatures
reconsider.respond: Check if Hammox adapts to new type syntax; if not, plain Mox + compiler types is the path

variants.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
variants.chosen_option: Hand-rolled mock modules - `LLM.Mock` and `LLM.ErrorMock` implement the LLM behaviour, swapped via `Application.get_env` config

reconsider.observe: Test suite grows large enough that `async: false` becomes a bottleneck
reconsider.respond: Revisit Mox migration to enable async test execution

reconsider.observe: A bug slips through because a mock returned data for the wrong input
reconsider.respond: Mox expectations would have caught the argument mismatch

reconsider.observe: Need a third mock behavior (e.g., partial success)
reconsider.respond: Creating yet another module is a sign the hand-rolled approach doesn't scale

variants.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
variants.chosen_option: Migrate to Mox - define a Mox mock from the LLM behaviour, use per-test expectations, remove hand-rolled mock modules

reconsider.observe: Only one external dependency (LLM) needs mocking; Mox overhead not justified
reconsider.respond: If a second external dependency appears (e.g., email, storage), Mox pays for itself immediately

reconsider.observe: Mox expectations make tests brittle by coupling to internal call structure
reconsider.respond: Use `Mox.stub/3` for tests that only care about output, `expect` for tests that verify integration

variants.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
variants.chosen_option: Do nothing - no mocking; tests call the real Anthropic API

reconsider.observe: This is the starting point before any mocking exists
reconsider.respond: Any project with external API calls should introduce mocking early

flat.scenario: Which HTTP client should we use to call the Anthropic API from Elixir?
flat.chosen_option: Req: modern Elixir HTTP client, declarative API, built-in JSON handling

reconsider.observe: Anthropic API changes (new models, new message format)
reconsider.respond: Thin wrapper means changes are localized to llm.ex; update in one place

flat.scenario: When multiple participants submit scenario reframings in rapid succession, should each submission trigger a separate LLM synthesis call, or should we debounce?
flat.chosen_option: 800ms debounce: each new submission resets the timer; LLM call fires only after 800ms of quiet; managed via `{:debounce, key, delay_ms, call_spec}` effect

reconsider.observe: 800ms feels sluggish when only one participant is submitting
reconsider.respond: Reduce to 300-500ms; or fire immediately if only one connected user has submitted

reconsider.observe: Debounce timer accumulates across stages (key collision)
reconsider.respond: Namespace keys by stage or clear all debounce timers on stage transition

flat.scenario: How do we test decision flows that trigger LLM calls without making real API requests, while keeping the LLM integration swappable?
flat.chosen_option: Elixir `@behaviour` on the LLM module; `MaudeLibs.LLM.Mock` implements canned responses; Server reads the module from application config at startup

reconsider.observe: Need to test error paths (LLM returns {:error, ...})
reconsider.respond: Add Mock variants or make Mock configurable per-test via process dictionary or Agent

reconsider.observe: Mock responses are too simplistic; don't exercise JSON parsing edge cases
reconsider.respond: Make Mock responses match exact API response shape, including nested structures

flat.scenario: Should the scaffolding LLM call (generating for/against points per option) be one call for all options or one call per option?
flat.chosen_option: Single call: all options + priorities in one request; output array of {name, for, against} per option

reconsider.observe: One call times out due to large option count
reconsider.respond: Split into N parallel calls (one per option) and merge results

flat.scenario: How do we ensure LLM responses are machine-parseable JSON that maps predictably to our Elixir structs?
flat.chosen_option: Prompt coercion: system prompt instructs model to return only raw JSON matching the specified schema; no tool use

reconsider.observe: Parse failures become frequent (>5% of calls)
reconsider.respond: Switch to Anthropic tool use / function calling for guaranteed JSON schema enforcement

flat.scenario: How do invited participants discover and navigate to a decision they've been invited to join?
flat.chosen_option: Per-user PubSub topic (`"user:#{username}"`) delivers invite notifications; canvas shows a modal; creator uses add/remove list UI with UserRegistry autocomplete

reconsider.observe: Users miss invites because they're not on /canvas
reconsider.respond: Add a persistent "pending invites" badge visible from /d/:id

flat.scenario: How does the invite UI know which usernames exist in the system so the creator can autocomplete when inviting participants?
flat.chosen_option: ETS-backed GenServer that registers every username on /join; `list_usernames/0` feeds an HTML `<datalist>` for autocomplete

reconsider.observe: Stale usernames clutter autocomplete after many sessions
reconsider.respond: Add TTL-based cleanup (e.g., discard entries older than 24 hours)

reconsider.observe: Need to associate persistent data with users (history, preferences)
reconsider.respond: Graduate to a real user table with Ecto

flat.scenario: How does the Decision.Server broadcast state changes to all connected LiveViews for a given decision?
flat.chosen_option: Broadcast full Decision struct on every state change; LiveViews assign and re-render

reconsider.observe: Broadcast payload becomes large (many options with long for/against text)
reconsider.respond: Switch to broadcasting only the changed stage field or a diff

flat.scenario: How do we identify participants across LiveView connections without building an authentication system?
flat.chosen_option: Username stored in Plug session via POST /session; session cookie persists the identity; no database, no accounts, no passwords

reconsider.observe: Username collisions cause confusion in decisions
reconsider.respond: Add uniqueness check against UserRegistry; show error on /join if taken

reconsider.observe: Need to persist decisions across sessions (e.g., "my decisions" page)
reconsider.respond: Add lightweight auth (magic link email, or OAuth) and associate decisions with accounts

flat.scenario: How do non-participants (observers, dropped users) view a decision in progress without disrupting it?
flat.chosen_option: Same decision_live.ex; check `username in decision.connected` to gate input controls; spectators see all but can't interact

reconsider.observe: Spectators want to interact (e.g., ask to join)
reconsider.respond: Add a "request to join" mechanic that creator can approve

flat.scenario: How do we structure the decision state machine so it is testable, predictable, and easy to reason about under concurrent LiveView connections?
flat.chosen_option: Pure Core + GenServer Shell: Core.handle/2 returns {:ok, decision, [effects]}, Server GenServer executes effects

reconsider.observe: Effect list grows complex (debounce + async_llm + broadcast in one transition)
reconsider.respond: Add effect priority ordering or a dedicated effect pipeline module

flat.scenario: When a participant's LiveView disconnects (page refresh, network hiccup, tab switch on mobile), should they be immediately removed from the connected set, or should there be a grace window for reconne
flat.chosen_option: 5-second grace period: Server schedules a `{:disconnect_timeout, user}` message on disconnect; reconnect within 5s cancels the timer; configurable via `disconnect_grace_ms`

reconsider.observe: 5 seconds feels too long; ghost presence confuses other participants
reconsider.respond: Reduce to 2-3 seconds; or add a visual "reconnecting..." indicator during grace period

reconsider.observe: 5 seconds feels too short; mobile users on slow networks time out
reconsider.respond: Increase to 10 seconds; consider exponential backoff for repeated disconnects

flat.scenario: How do we handle a participant's LiveView disconnecting mid-decision without blocking the remaining participants?
flat.chosen_option: connected + participants MapSets on Decision struct; terminate/2 triggers a 5-second grace period before removal; all ready-up checks use connected; participants persists across reconnects

reconsider.observe: A decision gets stuck because all participants disconnected
reconsider.respond: Add a timeout or allow the last connected user to auto-advance after N minutes

flat.scenario: How do we represent the current stage of a decision and its stage-specific data in Elixir?
flat.chosen_option: Tagged struct per stage: %Stage.Lobby{}, %Stage.Scenario{}, etc., stored in Decision.stage

reconsider.observe: Stage structs need versioning for live deploys
reconsider.respond: Add a vsn field or use a registry-based approach

flat.scenario: How many machines should we run on fly.io, and do we need BEAM clustering for LiveView state?
flat.chosen_option: Single machine on fly.io (sjc region); auto-stop/auto-start; no clustering needed; all GenServer state on one node

reconsider.observe: Demo requires high availability or multiple concurrent groups
reconsider.respond: Add libcluster + Horde for distributed GenServer registry; configure fly.io with 2+ machines and BEAM clustering

flat.scenario: Should decision state be persisted to a database, ETS, or live only in GenServer memory?
flat.chosen_option: Ephemeral GenServers: all decision state in memory; accept state loss on restart for prototype scope

reconsider.observe: Demo requires state to survive deploys (multi-day event)
reconsider.respond: Add ETS persistence with :ets.tab2file on terminate and reload on start; or add Postgres/Ecto

flat.scenario: How do we position decision circles on the lobby canvas and participant cards on stage views so that layouts are non-overlapping, visually balanced, responsive to viewport size, and work within LiveVi
flat.chosen_option: Three-layer architecture: (1) CSS fixed shell for persistent navigation, (2) D3 force simulation in JS hooks for position computation, (3) scale-to-fit transform on a virtual canvas for viewport adaptation. Two distinct D3 hooks serve the lobby canvas and stage views respectively.

reconsider.observe: LiveView morphdom patches cause visible position flicker despite StageForce.updated()
reconsider.respond: Add CSS transitions to card wrappers, or debounce re-apply

reconsider.observe: Stage participant count exceeds 6-8 and arc placement gets crowded
reconsider.respond: Switch stage simulation to use forceCollide in addition to forceX/forceY

reconsider.observe: Text becomes unreadable below 0.5x scale on small viewports
reconsider.respond: Add mobile-specific layout that stacks cards vertically instead of scaling

reconsider.observe: Want server-authoritative positions (e.g., screenshots)
reconsider.respond: Add periodic position sync from client back to server

flat.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
flat.chosen_option: Use d3-force in a LiveView JS hook for layout computation, with libgraph as the server-side graph data structure. Server sends node metadata (size, visibility, fixed position) via push_event; D3 handles physics and rendering.

reconsider.observe: LiveView hook lifecycle issues cause layout glitches on reconnect
reconsider.respond: Push full node list on mounted; hook re-runs simulation from scratch (fast - sub-second)

reconsider.observe: Need server-authoritative positions for a feature (e.g., server-side screenshot)
reconsider.respond: Add a periodic position sync from client back to server, or accept that screenshots show initial positions

reconsider.observe: Want to add edges between decisions later (e.g., dependencies)
reconsider.respond: libgraph on server models edges; pass links array to D3; add `forceLink()` to simulation

flat.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
flat.chosen_option: Use d3-force via a Node.js port/NIF called from the server, broadcasting final positions via PubSub; client only animates

reconsider.observe: Port communication latency exceeds the 1500ms tick budget
reconsider.respond: Move to client-side d3-force; server only tracks circle metadata

reconsider.observe: Node.js process crashes or hangs
reconsider.respond: Add supervision and restart logic; consider fallback to simple grid layout

flat.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
flat.chosen_option: Do nothing - keep the current hand-rolled server-side force simulation

reconsider.observe: A simple constant tweak finally produces stable behavior at 12+ nodes
reconsider.respond: Keep current approach; document the working constants

flat.scenario: Which approach should we use to compute non-overlapping, center-clustered positions for decision circles on the canvas homepage, given that the current hand-rolled server-side simulation oscillates an
flat.chosen_option: Rewrite the pure Elixir force layout as a tested, idempotent pure function using the spring-electrical model with adaptive step size and early termination

reconsider.observe: Tests pass but visual result looks wrong (clumped, uneven)
reconsider.respond: The force balance is wrong for this aesthetic goal; switch to d3-force client-side

reconsider.observe: O(n^2) computation exceeds tick budget at 20+ nodes
reconsider.respond: Reduce iterations or switch to client-side computation

reconsider.observe: Convergence tests are flaky due to floating point edge cases
reconsider.respond: Increase convergence threshold or use approximate equality

flat.scenario: When multiple participants submit priorities or options, when should each participant's input become visible to the others?
flat.chosen_option: All inputs visible live as you type; confirm button separates "I'm done" from "I'm ready to advance"

reconsider.observe: Users complain of anchoring - their inputs are shaped by seeing others first
reconsider.respond: Switch to rolling reveal: hide inputs until user has confirmed their own entry

flat.scenario: How should Claude's suggested priorities and options be presented and adopted by participants?
flat.chosen_option: Toggle model: anyone can include/exclude a suggestion; last write wins; broadcast immediately

reconsider.observe: Last-write-wins causes confusion when two users rapidly toggle the same suggestion
reconsider.respond: Add optimistic locking or switch to majority vote (2/4 participants = include)

flat.scenario: How do participants agree on the final scenario framing before advancing to priorities?
flat.chosen_option: Unanimous vote: all connected participants must select the same candidate to advance

reconsider.observe: Participants are stuck and can't reach unanimity
reconsider.respond: Add a "creator override" escape hatch or a majority vote fallback after N minutes

flat.scenario: How should we structure UI components across the decision stages - reusable LiveComponents, inline function components, or duplication?
flat.chosen_option: Inline function components (attr + slot, no stateful LiveComponent) for shared layout; per-stage inline heex for unique mechanics

reconsider.observe: A component needs its own event handling (e.g., inline modal with close button)
reconsider.respond: Promote to stateful LiveComponent at that point only

flat.scenario: Which CSS approach should we use for styling the Phoenix LiveView app?
flat.chosen_option: Tailwind CSS + DaisyUI: Phoenix ships Tailwind by default; DaisyUI adds component classes (card, btn, badge, input) with custom dark/light themes in oklch

reconsider.observe: Tailwind class names become unwieldy on complex components
reconsider.respond: Extract to Phoenix LiveComponents with @apply in component CSS files

flat.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
flat.chosen_option: Use both Dialyzer and the gradual type system during the transition period - maximum coverage now, converge to compiler-only when gradual types mature

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) covers cross-dependency inference and typed structs
reconsider.respond: Dialyzer is now redundant; drop Dialyxir, migrate `@spec` to new type signatures

reconsider.observe: The two tools produce conflicting or confusing feedback on the same code
reconsider.respond: Prioritize compiler warnings; Dialyzer becomes supplementary

reconsider.observe: Maintaining `@spec` annotations for Dialyzer while the gradual system infers types feels like double work
reconsider.respond: Stop adding new `@spec` annotations; let existing ones serve Dialyzer until it's dropped

flat.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
flat.chosen_option: Add `@spec`/`@type` annotations and run Dialyxir (`mix dialyzer`) for static type analysis

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) covers everything Dialyzer does at compile time
reconsider.respond: Drop Dialyxir dependency, migrate `@spec` to new type signature syntax, rely on compiler

reconsider.observe: Dialyzer misses a bug that the gradual type system would have caught
reconsider.respond: Accept the limitation or supplement with the gradual type system in parallel

reconsider.observe: PLT builds slow down the development workflow
reconsider.respond: Only run Dialyzer in precommit/CI, not on every save

flat.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
flat.chosen_option: Lean into the compiler's gradual type system - let `mix compile` catch type errors, skip Dialyzer entirely

reconsider.observe: v1.20 adds cross-dependency inference and guard types
reconsider.respond: Review warnings on upgrade; may surface new type issues in existing code

reconsider.observe: v1.21+ adds user-facing type signatures
reconsider.respond: Begin annotating public APIs with the new syntax for documentation and enforcement

reconsider.observe: The gradual type system misses bugs that Dialyzer would catch
reconsider.respond: Consider adding Dialyzer as a supplement until the gradual system reaches parity

flat.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
flat.chosen_option: Do nothing - continue with `@callback` on the LLM behaviour and no other type annotations or checking

reconsider.observe: A type-related bug reaches production that specs or the compiler would have caught
reconsider.respond: Adopt at least `@spec` annotations on public APIs

reconsider.observe: Elixir's gradual type system reaches maturity (v1.21+)
reconsider.respond: Adopt it with minimal migration cost since there's nothing to convert

flat.scenario: How should we utilize Elixir's type system to improve code quality and catch bugs?
flat.chosen_option: Add `@type` and `@spec` annotations to public functions for documentation, without running any enforcement tool

reconsider.observe: Specs drift from actual code because nothing enforces them
reconsider.respond: Add Dialyzer to catch spec violations

reconsider.observe: Gradual type system (v1.21+) introduces new type signature syntax
reconsider.respond: Migrate `@spec` annotations to the new syntax; specs-only approach makes this straightforward

flat.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
flat.chosen_option: Set threshold to 100% and add stage component modules to `ignore_modules`, unignoring them temporarily when modifying their code.

reconsider.observe: Elixir's cover tool fixes the `defmodule` counting
reconsider.respond: Remove stage components from ignore_modules; 100% is achievable natively

reconsider.observe: Developers keep forgetting the unignore/re-ignore step and real gaps slip through
reconsider.respond: Drop to 99% threshold and remove stage components from ignore_modules

reconsider.observe: New stage components are added and the ignore_modules list keeps growing
reconsider.respond: Evaluate whether the bookkeeping cost outweighs the benefit of the 100% headline number

reconsider.observe: A CI script could automate the unignore check (run coverage once without ignore for each changed module)
reconsider.respond: Build the automation; this eliminates L2 workflow friction and L1 blind spot risk

flat.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
flat.chosen_option: Set threshold to 99% and accept the `defmodule` gap without workarounds.

reconsider.observe: Elixir's cover tool fixes the `defmodule` counting (e.g., stops marking it as a relevant line)
reconsider.respond: Bump threshold to 100%; the gap closes naturally

reconsider.observe: More modules with `defmodule`-only gaps accumulate, eroding the 99% buffer
reconsider.respond: Either add them to ignore_modules or lower threshold further - but this signals a code organization issue, not a coverage issue

reconsider.observe: A real bug hides in the 0.76% headroom
reconsider.respond: Tighten the threshold or switch to the 100% + ignore_modules approach

flat.scenario: What level of line coverage enforcement should we adopt for our test suite, and how should we handle coverage tool limitations that make 100% unachievable without workarounds?
flat.chosen_option: Do nothing - no coverage threshold enforcement.

reconsider.observe: A bug ships to production that would have been caught by even basic coverage enforcement
reconsider.respond: Revisit adding a threshold, even a low one like 80%

reconsider.observe: Test suite grows organically and maintains high coverage through culture alone
reconsider.respond: Status quo is working; formalize with a threshold only if drift appears

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Use Wallaby (or Playwright via ports) to drive real browser sessions through the full multi-user UI workflow.

reconsider.observe: Browser tests take >30s and block CI
reconsider.respond: Move to a separate "e2e" CI job that runs on merge only, not on every push

reconsider.observe: Tests break on every UI change despite behavior being correct
reconsider.respond: Switch to data-testid selectors and consider replacing with LiveViewTest for non-visual assertions

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Dev seed routes for instant manual stage access + LiveView integration tests for automated multi-user regression coverage. Skip browser automation to avoid brittleness.

reconsider.observe: JS hook or canvas bugs keep slipping through
reconsider.respond: Add targeted Wallaby tests for JS-heavy features only (not full flow coverage)

reconsider.observe: Seed route state shapes drift from reality after Core refactors
reconsider.respond: Add a test that validates each builder produces a state that Core.handle/2 accepts

reconsider.observe: Builder module grows complex with many stage permutations
reconsider.respond: Consider a pipeline API: `Builders.new(users) |> Builders.advance_to(:priorities)`

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Dev-only seed routes that programmatically create decisions pre-advanced to any stage with fake users already joined.

reconsider.observe: You find yourself manually testing the same flow repeatedly even with seeds
reconsider.respond: Add LiveView integration tests for that flow

reconsider.observe: Seed routes drift from real stage shapes after a Core refactor
reconsider.respond: Share builders between seeds and tests

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Use Phoenix.LiveViewTest to mount LiveViews in-process, simulate multi-user flows, and assert stage transitions without a real browser.

reconsider.observe: JS hooks or canvas interactions cause bugs that LiveViewTest can't catch
reconsider.respond: Add targeted Wallaby/Playwright tests for JS-heavy features only

reconsider.observe: Test setup for multi-user flows is too verbose
reconsider.respond: Extract shared builder/helper modules (similar to existing `decision()` helpers in core_test)

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Do nothing; continue with manual browser testing and existing Core unit tests.

reconsider.observe: Time spent manually testing exceeds time you'd spend writing test infrastructure
reconsider.respond: Adopt one of the other variants

reconsider.observe: A regression ships that automated tests would have caught
reconsider.respond: Prioritize automated coverage

flat.scenario: How can we increase verification feedback and reduce the cost of testing multi-user, multi-stage decision workflows during development?
flat.chosen_option: Use Tidewave's MCP tools (project_eval, browser_eval, get_logs) as a live agentic verification layer during Claude Code sessions, complementing automated tests and seed routes.

reconsider.observe: You find yourself re-doing the same Tidewave verification steps across sessions
reconsider.respond: Encode those steps as LiveView integration tests so they persist

reconsider.observe: Tidewave snapshots become the only verification and tests are neglected
reconsider.respond: Tidewave is complementary, not a replacement. Run mix test before committing.

reconsider.observe: A new team member can't reproduce verification steps from a previous session
reconsider.respond: Document common verification patterns in a dev guide, or encode as tests

flat.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
flat.chosen_option: Migrate to Hammox - Mox with automatic typespec validation on mock return values via `@callback` specs

reconsider.observe: Server test suite grows large and `async: false` with `set_mox_global` becomes a bottleneck
reconsider.respond: Refactor test GenServer startup to use `Mox.allow/3` or start GenServer directly under test process, enabling per-process isolation and `async: true`

reconsider.observe: Elixir's gradual type system (v1.21+) covers `@callback` type signatures at compile time
reconsider.respond: Drop Hammox, swap `Hammox.expect` back to `Mox.expect`; the compiler now catches return type mismatches before tests even run. Hammox was a bridge for the gap year before compiler-native type checking.

reconsider.observe: Hammox maintenance lags behind Mox releases
reconsider.respond: Fall back to plain Mox; lose typespec validation but keep all other benefits

reconsider.observe: `@callback` specs are being phased out in favor of new type signatures
reconsider.respond: Check if Hammox adapts to new type syntax; if not, plain Mox + compiler types is the path

flat.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
flat.chosen_option: Hand-rolled mock modules - `LLM.Mock` and `LLM.ErrorMock` implement the LLM behaviour, swapped via `Application.get_env` config

reconsider.observe: Test suite grows large enough that `async: false` becomes a bottleneck
reconsider.respond: Revisit Mox migration to enable async test execution

reconsider.observe: A bug slips through because a mock returned data for the wrong input
reconsider.respond: Mox expectations would have caught the argument mismatch

reconsider.observe: Need a third mock behavior (e.g., partial success)
reconsider.respond: Creating yet another module is a sign the hand-rolled approach doesn't scale

flat.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
flat.chosen_option: Migrate to Mox - define a Mox mock from the LLM behaviour, use per-test expectations, remove hand-rolled mock modules

reconsider.observe: Only one external dependency (LLM) needs mocking; Mox overhead not justified
reconsider.respond: If a second external dependency appears (e.g., email, storage), Mox pays for itself immediately

reconsider.observe: Mox expectations make tests brittle by coupling to internal call structure
reconsider.respond: Use `Mox.stub/3` for tests that only care about output, `expect` for tests that verify integration

flat.scenario: Which mock implementation strategy should we use for isolating external dependencies (LLM calls) in tests: hand-rolled mock modules or a library like Mox?
flat.chosen_option: Do nothing - no mocking; tests call the real Anthropic API

reconsider.observe: This is the starting point before any mocking exists
reconsider.respond: Any project with external API calls should introduce mocking early
